title: NOTAM Management
summary: Glidepath fetches live FAA NOTAMs on demand via a server-side proxy, caches results for 5 minutes, and uses a polling hook to feed expiring-soon counts to the sidebar badge.
concept:
What Are NOTAMs in Glidepath?

NOTAMs (Notices to Air Missions) in Glidepath come from two sources: the live FAA feed and local NOTAMs stored in the notams table. The module at /notams primarily surfaces the live FAA feed — it queries https://notams.aim.faa.gov/notamSearch/search on demand and normalizes the results into a consistent shape. There is no local-NOTAM creation workflow in the current codebase.

FAA Feed Architecture

The page calls GET /api/notams/sync?icao=KSEM. The route handler (app/api/notams/sync/route.ts) does the following:

  • Validates the ICAO code (3–4 uppercase letters).
  • Checks the per-IP rate limit: max 120 requests per hour using the Postgres-backed sliding-window limiter in lib/rate-limit.ts. The check fails open if the limiter is unavailable.
  • POSTs to the FAA endpoint with searchType=0&designatorsForLocation=KSEM.
  • Uses Next.js fetch caching: next: { revalidate: 300 } — the CDN/Edge cache serves the same ICAO result for up to 5 minutes without a new upstream call.
  • Normalizes each FaaNotamItem into a flat shape with id, notam_number, source: 'faa', status, notam_type, full_text, effective_start, effective_end.
  • Derives status: 'expired' from both the FAA's cancelledOrExpired flag and by parsing the endDate string (MM/DD/YYYY HHMM format) and comparing to current UTC.

Expiring-Soon Detection

The hook lib/use-expiring-notams.ts exports useExpiringNotamCount(). It calls /api/notams/sync once on mount and then every 5 minutes via setInterval. For each active NOTAM it parses the effective_end field using the same parseFaaDate() helper and checks whether the difference from Date.now() is between 0 and 24 hours. The resulting count drives the sidebar badge dot.

Client-Side Display Logic

The page (app/(app)/notams/page.tsx) keeps the FAA ICAO search bar, a color-coded connection status card (green/red/gray dot), and a 5-filter chip row: All, FAA, LOCAL, Active, Expired. Each NOTAM card has:

  • A color-coded left border rule: red for safety (NOTAM number starts with M) or expiring soon, gray for expired, cyan for normal FAA, purple for local.
  • An EXPIRING SOON badge and a red glow when within 24 hours of expiry.
  • An SAFETY badge when the NOTAM number prefix is M.

Date Formatting

The compactNotamDate() helper formats timestamps for narrow card grids: within ±1 day it renders Today 1200Z / Tomorrow 0800Z; same year omits the year (Mar 9 0800Z); different year includes it (Mar 9, 2027 0800Z).

PDF Export

The Export PDF button generates a landscape jsPDF autoTable with 7 columns. Rows with a NOTAM number starting with M receive a light-red fill; rows starting with L receive a light-yellow fill. The export also supports email delivery via sendPdfViaEmail().

Demo Mode

When Supabase is not configured (createClient() returns null), the page renders DEMO_NOTAMS from lib/demo-data.ts and hides the ICAO search bar and refresh button.

code.caption: Server-side FAA NOTAM proxy — rate limit, 5-min cache, normalization
code.note: Whole file is the FAA feed integration: validates ICAO, rate-limits per-IP, POSTs to FAA, caches 300s, maps FaaNotamItem to a normalized shape.

code.caption: useExpiringNotamCount hook — polls every 5 minutes for sidebar badge
code.note: Calls the same /api/notams/sync endpoint; counts active NOTAMs whose effective_end is 0–24 hours out.

code.caption: NOTAM list page — filter chips, expiring-soon cards, export/email
code.from: /** Check if a NOTAM is expiring within 24 hours */
code.note: isExpiringSoon() and compactNotamDate() are the key client-side date helpers.

code.caption: NOTAM card rendering — color-coded left rule, SAFETY and EXPIRING SOON badges

code.note: leftRuleColor derives from expiringSoon, isSafety, isExpired, and source.

fits:
The NOTAM module is a read-only FAA window plus a sidebar notification bridge. It connects to the rest of the platform in three ways:

  • Sidebar badge — useExpiringNotamCount() feeds the useSidebarBadgeCounts hook, giving Airfield Managers a visible count of NOTAMs expiring within 24 hours.
  • Rate limiting — shares the same lib/rate-limit.ts sliding-window infrastructure used by the forgot-password, signup-email, and elevation routes to protect upstream third-party APIs.
  • PDF/Email pattern — the Export PDF + email workflow mirrors the pattern used by the Checks, Inspections, Discrepancy, and PPR modules: jsPDF client-side generation → sendPdfViaEmail() → /api/send-pdf-email (Resend).

quiz.q: The /api/notams/sync route uses `next: { revalidate: 300 }` on its fetch call. What is the practical effect of this in a Vercel deployment?
choices: The server pauses for 300 seconds before responding to each request
Next.js caches the FAA response at the CDN/Edge layer and serves it without a new upstream FAA call for up to 5 minutes
The route handler is re-executed every 300 seconds on a cron schedule
Client-side fetch caches the result in localStorage for 5 minutes
quiz.explain: The `next: { revalidate: N }` option in Next.js fetch instructs the framework (and Vercel's cache) to serve the stale cached response for up to N seconds before re-fetching from the upstream URL. This is an incremental static regeneration (ISR) strategy applied to route handlers, meaning the FAA endpoint is not hammered on every request.

quiz.q: The rate limiter in /api/notams/sync is called with `checkRateLimits(admin, [...])` using the `getAdminClient()` service-role client. Why can't the anon client be used here?
choices: The anon client lacks permission to call the check_rate_limit RPC, which is granted only to service_role and authenticated roles
The anon client cannot make POST requests
The anon client does not have network access to the rate_limit_hits table
The RPC requires a JWT that the anon client does not carry
quiz.explain: The SECURITY DEFINER `check_rate_limit` RPC is explicitly granted to `service_role` and `authenticated`, and EXECUTE is never granted to `anon`. The NOTAM sync route is unauthenticated (no session cookie), so only the service-role admin client can call the RPC. The check fails open if `getAdminClient()` returns null (e.g., env vars missing).

quiz.q: A NOTAM card shows a red left border and an EXPIRING SOON badge. What TWO conditions must both be true at the same time for this to render?
choices: status === 'active' AND effective_end is between 0 and 24 hours from now
source === 'faa' AND notam_number starts with 'E'
status === 'expired' AND the card index is less than 5
notam_type === 'SAFETY' AND effective_end is PERM
quiz.explain: isExpiringSoon() returns true only when the NOTAM is NOT expired and the time difference between effective_end and Date.now() is greater than 0 and at most 24 * 60 * 60 * 1000 ms. In the card render, `expiringSoon = !isExpired && isExpiringSoon(notam.effective_end)`, so both conditions — still active AND within the 24-hour window — must hold.

title: Obstruction Evaluations
summary: Glidepath implements a full UFC 3-260-01 (USAF) and FAA Part 77 imaginary-surface evaluation engine using geodesic coordinate math, fetching ground elevation via the Google Elevation API, to determine whether an object violates airfield airspace criteria.
concept:
What the Module Does

The Obstruction Evaluations module (/obstructions) lets an Airfield Manager pin any point on the airfield map, enter an object height, and get an instant determination of which UFC 3-260-01 or FAA Part 77 imaginary surfaces the object penetrates. Results include the controlling surface (lowest allowable ceiling the object is inside), penetration depth in feet, step-by-step calculation breakdowns, and waiver guidance.

Surface Sets — UFC vs FAA Part 77

The engine supports two surface sets, selected by the bases.obstruction_surface_set column ('ufc_3_260_01' or 'faa_part77'):

UFC 3-260-01 (USAF default)FAA Part 77 (civilian/Part 139)
Primary, Approach-Departure (50:1), Transitional (7:1), Inner Horizontal (150 ft / 13,120 ft radius), Conical (20:1 / 7,000 ft), Outer Horizontal (500 ft / 42,250 ft radius), Clear Zone, Graded Area, APZ I, APZ IIPrimary (width varies by approach type), Approach (slope/length vary), Transitional (7:1), Horizontal (150 ft), Conical (20:1)

The FAA set has 6 approach-type variants (utility_visual through non_utility_precision) stored in PART77_DIMENSIONS. The precision-instrument approach encodes a two-segment slope: 50:1 for the first 10,000 ft, then 40:1 for the next 40,000 ft.

Geodesic Engine (lib/calculations/geometry.ts)

All geometry uses Haversine / great-circle math — no flat-earth approximation for the large UFC surfaces (the outer horizontal extends 42,250 ft, about 8 miles). Key functions:

  • offsetPoint(origin, bearingDeg, distFt) — great-circle offset; returns new LatLon.
  • distanceFt(a, b) — Haversine distance in feet.
  • bearing(a, b) — true bearing; always computed from actual endpoint coordinates rather than the stored true_heading (which is rounded to whole degrees, causing up to ~70 ft cross-track error on a 10,000 ft runway).
  • pointToRunwayRelation(point, rwy, opts?) — projects the point onto a local XY coordinate frame (origin = runway midpoint, X = along-track, Y = cross-track) and returns distanceFromCenterline, alongTrackFromMidpoint, withinPrimary, nearerEnd, and distanceFromNearestPrimaryEnd. The opts.primaryHalfWidth knob lets Part 77 callers supply the per-approach-type half-width (125–500 ft) rather than the UFC default of 1,000 ft.
  • fetchElevation(point) — proxies to /api/elevation, which calls the Google Elevation API.

Evaluation Engine (lib/calculations/obstructions.ts)

evaluateObstruction(point, heightAGL, groundElevMSL, rwy, airfieldElev, runwayClass) performs the UFC analysis. Per-surface computation:

  • Primary: within the primary rectangle (runway length + 200 ft extensions, ±1,000 ft of centerline)? Any object above airfield elevation violates.
  • Approach-Departure: beyond the primary surface end, inside the expanding trapezoid (inner width 1,000 ft → outer 2,550 ft over 25,000 ft)? Max allowable height = threshold elevation + (distance / 50). Uses per-threshold MSL elevation when available.
  • Transitional: alongside the primary rectangle or alongside the approach trapezoid (up to 7,500 ft where 7:1 × distance = 150 ft)? Max height = airfield elevation + (distance from edge / 7).
  • Inner Horizontal: within the stadium-shaped 13,120 ft radius around the runway primary-surface ends but NOT inside a more-specific surface? Fixed 150 ft AGL ceiling.
  • Conical: between the inner horizontal outer edge and 7,000 ft beyond it? Rises at 20:1 starting from 150 ft.
  • Outer Horizontal: within the outer stadium (42,250 ft radius) beyond the conical? Fixed 500 ft AGL ceiling.
  • Clear Zone / Graded Area: rectangular zones extending from each threshold (3,000 ft and 1,000 ft respectively); zero height permitted.
  • APZ I / II: land-use zones only (no height restriction encoded); presence detection only.

After all surfaces are evaluated, the controlling surface is the height-restricted surface with the lowest allowable MSL ceiling that contains the point.

Multi-Runway Analysis

evaluateObstructionAllRunways() runs the single-runway evaluator against every runway geometry in the base config and merges results: the controlling surface is the most restrictive across all runways.

Ground Elevation

The page fetches ground elevation via fetchElevation(point), which calls /api/elevation?lat=&lon=. That route proxies to https://maps.googleapis.com/maps/api/elevation/json (Google Elevation API), rate-limited to 2,000 calls/hour per IP. The result is returned in meters and converted to feet (elevM * 3.28084).

Persistence

Saved evaluations are written to the obstruction_evaluations table via lib/supabase/obstructions.ts. The surface_set column is pinned at create time (even if the base admin later changes the surface-set preference), so the detail page legend remains accurate for historical records.

code.caption: IMAGINARY_SURFACES constant — UFC 3-260-01 surface definitions with references
code.note: Complete UFC surface catalog: names, criteria, UFC references, criteria text templates, and display colors.

code.caption: evaluateObstruction — UFC per-surface computation loop
code.note: Function signature, groundElev derivation, and the beginning of the primary-surface check. Shows how runway relation and airfield elevation are combined.

code.caption: Controlling surface selection and waiver guidance
code.note: After all surfaces are evaluated, controlling surface = lowest maxAllowableHeightMSL among applicable surfaces; waiver guidance lists required actions.

code.caption: pointToRunwayRelation — local-frame projection, withinPrimary, nearerEnd
code.note: Converts geodesic lat/lon to a runway-aligned XY frame (feet) for accurate cross-track and along-track distance calculations.

code.caption: UFC Class B criteria table — numeric dimensions for all 10 surfaces
code.note: SurfaceCriteria interface and the Class B / Army Class B lookup table used by getSurfaceCriteria(runwayClass).

fits:
The obstruction engine is a self-contained geodesic computation library that the UI consumes. It connects to the rest of Glidepath as follows:

  • Dual-mode surface sets — getSurfaceSet() from lib/airport-mode.ts reads the base's airport_type flag to select UFC vs FAA Part 77. The obstructions page reads this via useInstallation().
  • Discrepancy workflow — when a violation is detected, waiver guidance recommends submitting a CES work order. Airfield Managers can cross-reference open discrepancies in /discrepancies.
  • Waivers module — the permanent or temporary airspace-criteria waiver process described in the waiver guidance maps to the AF Form 505 waivers module (/waivers).
  • Elevation API — the same /api/elevation route and rate-limiting pattern (2,000/hr per IP, fails open) protects the Google Elevation API billing account, matching the pattern used for the NOTAM sync endpoint.
  • Obstruction PDF — lib/obstruction-pdf.ts generates a per-evaluation PDF using the same { doc, filename } return contract as the other 19 PDF generators.

quiz.q: The geometry helper bearing() computes the true bearing from actual endpoint coordinates rather than using the stored true_heading column. Why?
choices: The true_heading column stores magnetic heading, not true heading
The stored true_heading is rounded to whole degrees, causing up to ~70 ft cross-track error on a 10,000 ft runway
bearing() is faster than a database lookup
The ICAO standard requires computing bearing from endpoints, not headings
quiz.explain: The code comment in getRunwayGeometry() explains: 'Published true_heading is rounded to whole degrees, which causes up to ~70ft cross-track error on long runways (0.75° × 10,000ft).' Computing from actual endpoint lat/lon coordinates yields geodesic precision, which matters when checking whether an object is within a 1,000 ft primary surface half-width.

quiz.q: A point is 12,000 ft from the nearest primary-surface end center along the extended centerline, and 800 ft from centerline. The runway uses UFC Class B criteria. Which surface has the lowest max allowable MSL ceiling at this location?
choices: Approach-Departure Clearance Surface (50:1 slope)
Transitional Surface (7:1 slope from approach edge)
Inner Horizontal Surface (fixed 150 ft AGL)
Clear Zone (0 ft AGL)
quiz.explain: At 12,000 ft along the approach corridor and 800 ft from centerline, the point is inside the approach-departure trapezoid (inner width 1,000 ft expanding to 2,550 ft over 25,000 ft; at 12,000 ft the half-width is ~1,186 ft, which is >800 ft). Max height = threshold elevation + 12,000/50 = threshold + 240 ft. The inner horizontal grants 150 ft AGL from airfield elevation — which is typically much lower than threshold + 240 ft — so if the inner horizontal applies it would be the controlling surface. However, the engine marks the inner horizontal as NOT applicable when the point is already inside approach-departure or transitional; the approach-departure surface governs.

quiz.q: When the surface_set column is pinned at create time for a saved obstruction evaluation, what problem does that solve?
choices: It ensures the evaluation can be re-run later without an internet connection
It prevents the detail-page legend from showing the wrong surface colors if the base admin later changes the surface-set preference
It speeds up the evaluation by caching the surface dimensions
It allows multiple runway classes to be evaluated in parallel
quiz.explain: The comment in createObstructionEvaluation() explicitly states 'Pinned at create-time so the detail-page legend stays accurate even if admin later flips bases.obstruction_surface_set.' A historical UFC record evaluated under the 10 UFC surfaces would display incorrect data if the base later switched to FAA Part 77's 5 surfaces.

title: Regulatory Reference Library
summary: The References and Aircraft modules provide a searchable, offline-capable library of 70 regulations and 200+ aircraft characteristics, with cross-PDF full-text search backed by a Postgres RPC and an IndexedDB blob/text cache for offline use.
concept:
Three Reference Modules at a Glance

Three separate pages share the same conceptual purpose — making regulatory and technical data available without leaving the app:

RouteWhat It Contains
/regulations70 regulations (DAFMAN, UFC, AFI, FAR, etc.) + personal PDF uploads (My Documents tab)
/libraryPDF viewer overlay driven by the same PDFLibrary component, gated on library:view permission
/aircraft200+ aircraft characteristics from USACE TSC 13-2 / TSC 13-3 JSON, with ACN/PCN comparison panel

Regulations Data (lib/regulations-data.ts)

The static fallback contains 70 RegulationEntry objects across three tiers: Core Publications (3 DAFMANs that are the operational bibles), Direct References (27 publications explicitly cited in DAFMAN Vol. 1–3), and Cross-References (27 related standards). Each entry has:

  • reg_id — e.g. 'DAFMAN 13-204, Vol. 2' — also used as the PDF filename key (sanitizeRegId() converts to a safe filename).
  • source — 'usaf' | 'faa' | 'both' | 'icao' | 'ufc' — drives dual-mode filtering. USAF bases see USAF + UFC + ICAO + both; civilian bases see FAA + ICAO + both. Controlled by getRegSource(currentInstallation) from lib/airport-mode.ts.
  • pub_type, category, tags[] — for filter chips and full-text tag search.

When Supabase is configured, the page fetches from the regulations table and prefers DB rows over the static list (dbRegs !== null ? dbRegs : ALL_REGULATIONS). System administrators can add or delete regulation entries through the UI.

Cross-PDF Full-Text Search

Typing 2+ characters in the search box triggers a debounced 300 ms effect that calls the Postgres RPC search_all_pdfs(search_query, max_results: 30). This returns { file_name, page_number, headline } rows — the headline is a snippet from the indexed page text with matching terms bolded. Clicking a match opens the PDF viewer directly at the matching page number.

If the server returns no results (offline or empty pdf_text_pages table), the search falls back to an IndexedDB scan of the locally cached STORE_TEXT store, searching across all cached page text with a simple String.indexOf().

IndexedDB PDF Cache (lib/idb.ts)

The app uses a single IndexedDB database named aoms_pdf_cache (version 4) with six object stores:

  • blobs — official regulation PDF binary blobs (keyed by sanitized filename).
  • meta — metadata for cached PDFs (timestamp, size).
  • text_pages — extracted page text for official regulations (used by offline full-text search).
  • text_meta — extraction metadata.
  • user_blobs — user-uploaded personal PDFs.
  • user_text — extracted page text for personal PDFs.

All helpers (idbGet, idbSet, idbDelete, idbGetAll, idbGetAllKeys, idbClear) are simple Promise wrappers around the native IDBDatabase API. The singleton dbPromise pattern ensures only one database connection is open.

My Documents Tab (Personal PDFs)

The My Documents tab lets authenticated users upload their own PDFs (stored in the user-uploads Supabase Storage bucket) and view them in the same PDF viewer. lib/userDocuments.ts (userDocService) manages upload, list, cache, text extraction, and sync. Records are kept in a user_documents table with a status field: 'uploaded' → 'extracting' → 'ready' | 'failed'.

Aircraft Characteristics (lib/aircraft-data.ts)

Aircraft data is loaded from two static JSON files at build time: public/commercial_aircraft.json and public/military_aircraft.json (USACE TSC 13-2 / 13-3 sources). An image manifest (public/image_manifest.json) resolves silhouette URLs. The module exports:

  • allAircraft — merged and sorted array tagged with category: 'military' | 'commercial'.
  • AIRCRAFT_COUNT — total, commercial, military counts.
  • MANUFACTURERS — unique manufacturer list for filter chips.
  • getSortValue() — parses numeric strings from fields like wing_span_ft, max_to_wt_klbs, turn_radius_ft for sort comparisons.

The Aircraft page also includes an ACN/PCN comparison panel: given a pavement PCN value, pavement type (rigid/flexible), subgrade (A–D), and weight mode (max/min), it looks up the matching ACN value from the aircraft record and determines whether the aircraft exceeds the pavement rating.

Favorites and Offline State

Both the Regulations and Aircraft pages persist starred/favorite items to localStorage (separate keys per module). The Regulations page additionally persists a show favorites only default to localStorage so Airfield Managers who work from a personal shortlist survive page reloads.

fits:
The reference library modules are primarily read-only data surfaces, but they integrate with the rest of Glidepath in several ways:

  • Airport mode — getRegSource(currentInstallation) in lib/airport-mode.ts reads the same airport_type flag used throughout the platform (obstructions, training, help module, terminology). USAF bases see UFC/DAFMAN; civilian bases see FAA/AC.
  • Offline infrastructure — the IndexedDB PDF cache (lib/idb.ts) is part of the same offline-first architecture as the Workbox runtime caching added in the 2026-04-25 batch. The STORE_TEXT store makes full-text search possible even when the Supabase RPC is unreachable.
  • Permission gates — /library checks library:view via the user_has_permission RPC server-side (a server component making a single round-trip before rendering the client component). This is the same RPC that gates all other module features.
  • Aircraft / Obstructions pairing — ACN/PCN comparison in the aircraft module supplements the obstruction evaluations module: an Airfield Manager can confirm an aircraft's pavement rating before authorizing it to use a taxiway, then run an obstruction check for any equipment staging near that taxiway.

quiz.q: A USAF base and a civilian Part 139 airport both have the regulations page open. The USAF base sees DAFMAN 13-204 Vol. 2 but the civilian base does not. What controls this difference?
choices: The civilian base's profile has a permission override removing the regulation
getRegSource(currentInstallation) returns different sets of allowed source values based on airport_type, and DAFMAN 13-204 Vol. 2 has source: 'usaf'
The database query filters by base_id and only returns rows assigned to that base
The static ALL_REGULATIONS array is different for each deployment
quiz.explain: The useMemo block filters `raw` (DB rows or static array) by `allowed.has(r.source ?? 'usaf')`. `getRegSource()` returns `['usaf','ufc','icao','both']` for a USAF base and `['faa','icao','both']` for a civilian base. Since DAFMAN 13-204 Vol. 2 has `source: 'usaf'`, it passes the USAF filter but not the civilian one.

quiz.q: The cross-PDF search fires a Postgres RPC and falls back to an IDB scan. What is the minimum search term length that triggers either path?
choices: 1 character
2 characters
3 characters
Any non-empty string
quiz.explain: The useEffect guard is `if (search.trim().length < 2) { setContentResults([]); return }`. A single character clears results and returns immediately without calling the RPC or scanning IDB. Two or more characters start the 300 ms debounce timer.

quiz.q: The IndexedDB database is opened with a singleton dbPromise pattern. What problem does this solve in a React app with multiple components calling idbGet/idbSet concurrently?
choices: It prevents multiple IndexedDB databases from being created with the same name
It ensures only one IDBDatabase connection is established, avoiding redundant open requests and version conflict errors from concurrent upgrades
It speeds up all reads by pre-loading all stores into memory
It allows the database to be shared across browser tabs
quiz.explain: If every idbGet/idbSet call issued a new `indexedDB.open()`, multiple concurrent open requests could race during an `onupgradeneeded` event, leading to version conflicts or multiple redundant connections. The `if (dbPromise) return dbPromise` guard ensures the single Promise is reused — subsequent callers receive the same already-opened (or in-progress) connection.
